file_name: Fuentes et al. - 2024 - Convolutional neural network architecture for benign keratosis, melanocytic nevi and melanoma skin c.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture derived from VGG16 to classify benign keratosis (bkl), melanocytic nevi (nv), and melanoma skin cancer (mel) lesions. The study implements this deep learning model on the HAM10000 dataset to achieve an average classification accuracy of 79.94%, comparing its performance against pre-trained models like InceptionV3, ResNet50v2, and VGG16.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the design of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture derived from VGG16, its implementation on the HAM10000 dataset, and the reporting of new empirical results including classification accuracy (79.94%), precision, recall, and training time metrics compared against pre-trained models.

title: CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK ARCHITECTURE FOR BENIGN KERATOSIS, MELANOCYTIC NEVI AND MELANOMA SKIN CANCER DETECTION
brief_summary: This study presents a custom lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture derived from VGG16 to classify benign keratosis (bkl), melanocytic nevi (nv), and melanoma (mel) skin lesions. The model was trained and evaluated using a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset, achieving an average classification accuracy of 79.94%. The proposed architecture contains only 1.4 million trainable parameters, significantly fewer than the compared pre-trained models: InceptionV3 (103 million), ResNet50v2 (25.6 million), and VGG16 (138.4 million). Performance was assessed using precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy metrics on a test set comprising 491 training and 123 testing images per class. The results demonstrate that the proposed model offers competitive classification metrics while requiring considerably less computational processing time for training and evaluation compared to the pre-trained benchmarks. Consequently, the study concludes that the lightweight model is a promising approach for skin cancer detection due to its efficiency without penalizing diagnostic accuracy.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of pigmented skin lesions: benign keratosis (bkl), melanocytic nevi (nv), and melanoma (mel).
all_task_types.details: The paper implements a CNN classifier to predict one of three discrete classes (bkl, nv, mel) using a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset. The model uses a softmax activation function with 3 elements for the output layer. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics including accuracy (79.94%), precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset containing 10,015 images of 7 types of pigmented skin lesions. The study utilized a balanced subset of this dataset, specifically selecting non-repeated images for three classes to ensure equal representation.

use.training: Used to train the custom CNN model and pre-trained models (InceptionV3, ResNet50v2, VGG16) with 80% of the balanced subset (491 images per class).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper mentions validation accuracy during training epochs but does not define a distinct validation set count.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final performance of the classifiers with 20% of the balanced subset (123 images per class).
use.other: The full HAM10000 dataset (including akiec, bcc, df, vasc) was referenced to justify the selection of the three classes due to available image counts, but those classes were excluded from the experiments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed model and pre-trained models were based on descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and computational processing times without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000 (Balanced Subset: bkl, nv, mel).notes: The proposed model achieved competitive classification metrics (precision, recall, F1-score, accuracy) compared to pre-trained models while requiring significantly fewer trainable parameters (1.4M vs 25.6M-138.4M) and less computational processing time (82s vs 152s-241s). The validation accuracy for the proposed model stabilized after 15 epochs, whereas pre-trained models showed more instability or lower validation accuracy at epoch 20.

architecture_used.main_model: Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) derived from VGG16

preprocessing_techniques: Dataset balancing: Random selection of 614 non-repeated images per class (benign keratosis, melanocytic nevi, melanoma) from the HAM10000 dataset to create a balanced subset.
Image resizing: Input images resized to 150x150 pixels.
Color format: Images converted to RGB channel format.
conclusions: The proposed lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture, derived from VGG16, achieved an average classification accuracy of 79.94% for benign keratosis, melanocytic nevi, and melanoma skin lesions on a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset.

The proposed model is competitive in terms of classification metrics (precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy) compared to pre-trained models InceptionV3 (81.03%), ResNet50v2 (79.40%), and VGG16 (78.32%).
The proposed model requires significantly fewer trainable parameters (1.4 million) compared to InceptionV3 (103 million), ResNet50v2 (25.6 million), and VGG16 (138.4 million).

The proposed model demonstrated superior computational efficiency, requiring only 82 seconds for training and evaluation, which is considerably less than InceptionV3 (175 seconds), VGG16 (152 seconds), and ResNet50v2 (241 seconds).
The proposed model required only 34% of the processing time of ResNet50v2, attributed to its reduced number of trainable parameters.

The study concludes that the proposed lightweight model is a promising approach for skin cancer detection as it offers competitive diagnostic accuracy without penalizing performance while significantly reducing computational processing time.
important_remarks: The study uses a highly imbalanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset, artificially balancing it by selecting only 614 images per class (bkl, nv, mel) and excluding other clinically relevant classes (akiec, bcc, df, vasc), which limits generalizability to broader dermatological contexts.
No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the same HAM10000 dataset it was derived from, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific distribution.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed model and pre-trained baselines, relying solely on descriptive metrics.

The proposed model achieved lower accuracy (79.94%) than InceptionV3 (81.03%) on the test set, contradicting the claim of being 'competitive' without penalizing accuracy, although it offers significant computational efficiency gains.
Training and evaluation were conducted on Google Colab with a 12GB GPU, which may not reflect performance on lower-end or edge devices where lightweight models are typically deployed.

Data leakage risks are present due to the use of a 'balanced' subset created by random selection from the HAM10000 dataset without explicit mention of ensuring patient-level separation between training and testing sets.

The study excludes actinic keratosis (akiec), a precancerous lesion, which is critical for early melanoma detection workflows, limiting the clinical utility of the classifier for comprehensive skin cancer screening.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which may contribute to the relatively modest accuracy (79.94%) given the small dataset size (369 test images total).
